title: Evaluating Candidates
concept: Prompting a validator model to score each candidate step as promising, questionable, or dead-end.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: when Tree of Thoughts starts solving a problem, what does the model do first?

options.text: It writes one single answer and stops
It generates 3-5 candidate next steps to explore

exercises.explain: Tree of Thoughts begins by branching out: the model generates several candidate steps (usually 3-5) instead of committing to just one path.

cards.title: So many branches!
cards.body: You just grew 5 candidate steps. But some are smart and some are silly. Which ones deserve to keep growing?

cards.title: Evaluating candidates
cards.body: We ask a 'validator' model to score each candidate step. It labels them as promising, questionable, or dead-end. This helps us keep the good branches and prune the bad ones.

cards.title: A judge tasting dishes
cards.body: Imagine cooks each try a recipe step. A judge tastes each one and says 'yes, keep going', 'hmm, maybe', or 'no, throw it out'. The validator is that judge for your ideas.

cards.title: Scoring in action
cards.body: For a maze, one candidate move is scored 'promising' (heads toward the exit), one 'questionable' (unclear), and one 'dead-end' (walks into a wall). Only the promising path grows next.

cards.title: Myth: it needs a second AI
cards.body: You often use the SAME model to evaluate. You just give it a new prompt: 'Rate this step as promising, questionable, or dead-end.' It switches from creator to judge.

cards.title: Generate vs. Evaluate
cards.body: One step makes ideas, the next step scores them.

right.text: Score each: promising, questionable, or dead-end

exercises.prompt: What is the validator model's job in Tree of Thoughts?

options.text: To score each candidate step as promising, questionable, or dead-end
To generate brand-new problems to solve
To delete all candidate steps immediately
To translate the answer into another language

exercises.explain: The validator acts like a judge, rating each candidate step so the search can keep the strong branches and drop the weak ones.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A step labeled 'dead-end' should be pruned instead of explored further.
exercises.explain: A 'dead-end' label means the step leads nowhere useful, so we prune it and spend our effort on promising branches instead.

exercises.sentence: The validator marks a hopeful step as promising and a useless one as dead-end.
exercises.explain: 'Promising' is the label for a step that looks like it's heading toward a good solution, so we keep exploring it.

exercises.prompt: Which validator response was written by an AI following the scoring prompt?

options.text: Score: Promising. This step correctly narrows the search toward the goal and stays consistent with the earlier clues.
idk it's fine i guess whatever works lol
PIZZA TIME!!! who cares about the maze

exercises.explain: The AI validator gives a clear label ('Promising') plus a short reason tied to the task, exactly as the scoring prompt asks.

exercises.prompt: You generated 4 candidate steps for a puzzle. The validator scores them: A = promising, B = dead-end, C = questionable, D = dead-end. What is the smartest next move?

options.text: Explore A first (and maybe C), and prune B and D
Explore B and D because they're different
Keep all 4 equally to be safe
Throw away every candidate and start over

exercises.explain: Evaluating candidates lets you focus effort where it pays off: grow the 'promising' branch (A), maybe check the 'questionable' one (C), and prune the 'dead-ends' (B, D). This scoring step is what makes Tree of Thoughts smart instead of just guessing everywhere.
